---
title: "Interactive Visualizations"
jupyter: python3
format:
  html:
    toc: false
---

This page contains three interactive visualizations built from the final project dataset, satisfying the Homework 5 requirement, plus one supplementary model-diagnostic interactive figure.
The current data extract has full incident-day coverage across the analysis window (2014-01-01 to 2026-01-31).

## Figure 1: Delay Trend with Precipitation

This figure shows a daily time-series of TTC total delay minutes, with a 30-day moving average and daily precipitation overlay. The rolling trend indicates a gradual increase in delay burden over the full window, with visibly higher baseline levels in later years. Daily spikes remain episodic, and several high-precipitation periods align with elevated delay clusters.

<iframe src="outputs/viz1_timeseries.html" width="100%" height="560" style="border:2px solid #b7cae0; border-radius:6px;"></iframe>

**Caption.** Daily TTC delay minutes over time with a 30-day moving average and precipitation overlay.

## Figure 2: Monthly Mean Daily Delay (Year x Month)

This is a year-by-month heatmap of mean daily TTC delay minutes. The color field becomes generally darker from earlier-year rows to later-year rows (from bottom toward top), indicating higher monthly delay intensity in recent years. Within-year differences remain visible across months, but the multi-year darkening pattern is the dominant signal.

<iframe src="outputs/viz2_heatmap.html" width="100%" height="560" style="border:2px solid #b7cae0; border-radius:6px;"></iframe>

**Caption.** Monthly mean daily delay minutes arranged by year and month.

## Figure 3: Weekday vs Weekend Delay by Weather Type

This is a weekday-versus-weekend boxplot comparison within each weather category. Weekday distributions are centered above weekend distributions across categories, showing higher overall weekday delay burden. Under Heavy Rain/Snow, the weekend upper tail is long, but the weekday median remains clearly higher.

<iframe src="outputs/viz3_boxplot.html" width="100%" height="560" style="border:2px solid #b7cae0; border-radius:6px;"></iframe>

**Caption.** Boxplot comparison of daily delay minutes across weather categories by weekday/weekend (incident days only).

## Figure 4: ROC Curves for Severe-Day Classification (Supplementary)

This is an interactive ROC comparison across all four classification models. The Logistic Regression curve is highest overall and has the best ROC-AUC in this run, while the tree-based models are close but slightly lower. The figure shows moderate discrimination above random baseline rather than strong separation.

```{python}
from pathlib import Path
import pandas as pd
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from sklearn.metrics import roc_curve, auc

root = Path(".")
pred = pd.read_csv(root / "outputs" / "table_classification_predictions_test.csv")

roc_specs = [
    ("Logistic", "prob_logit", "#f28e2b"),
    ("Random Forest", "prob_rf", "#1f4e79"),
    ("XGBoost", "prob_xgb", "#7e57c2"),
    ("Decision Tree", "prob_dt", "#2a9d8f"),
]

fig = go.Figure()
for model_name, prob_col, color in roc_specs:
    fpr, tpr, _ = roc_curve(pred["actual_severe"], pred[prob_col])
    roc_auc = auc(fpr, tpr)
    fig.add_trace(
        go.Scatter(
            x=fpr,
            y=tpr,
            mode="lines",
            name=f"{model_name} (AUC={roc_auc:.3f})",
            line={"color": color, "width": 2.4},
        )
    )

fig.add_trace(
    go.Scatter(
        x=[0, 1],
        y=[0, 1],
        mode="lines",
        name="Random baseline",
        line={"color": "#8a8a8a", "width": 1.6, "dash": "dash"},
    )
)

fig.update_layout(
    template="plotly_white",
    margin={"l": 55, "r": 20, "t": 30, "b": 50},
    xaxis_title="False Positive Rate",
    yaxis_title="True Positive Rate",
    legend={"orientation": "h", "y": 1.12},
)
fig.update_xaxes(range=[0, 1])
fig.update_yaxes(range=[0, 1], scaleanchor="x", scaleratio=1)
fig
```

**Caption.** ROC-curve comparison for severe-day classification, with AUC shown in the legend.
